CLARITY.justification: Overall the paper follows a standard, logical structure (Intro → Related Work → Method → Experiments → Conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early with a bullet list. However presentation suffers from notable issues: variable/notation inconsistencies (e.g. w vs ω, inconsistent subscripting and spacing like s t and ∇f t), frequent grammatical/typographical problems (e.g. “unfordable”), and large blocks of repeated text that make reading noisy and confusing. Figures/tables are referenced and the algorithm pseudocode is useful, but the repeated/duplicated paragraphs and formatting errors reduce overall clarity and force re-reading of some sections.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper applies an actor-critic (RL) framework to the problem of learning per-step scalar learning rates for SGD, which is a concrete and reasonably novel application of existing RL techniques (Section 3: actor/critic setup; Algorithm 1). However, the problem of adaptive step-size and automatic learning-rate selection is well-studied (the authors cite Schaul et al. 2013, Daniel et al. 2016 and standard adaptive optimizers), and the method primarily combines known components (deterministic actor-critic, TD learning, LSTM state) with the specific twist of feeding different examples to actor and critic for better generalization (Section 3.4). Thus the contribution is an incremental, domain-specific extension of existing methods rather than a fundamentally new idea.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper clearly states an actor–critic method (Sec.3) and gives standard TD and deterministic policy‑gradient updates (Eqs.6–9) that are internally consistent; the algorithm is implemented (Alg.1) and evaluated on MNIST/CIFAR against common baselines and vSGD (Sec.4). However, experimental reporting lacks variance/error bars and statistical tests (only 5-run means reported), and some experimental/hyperparameter details for the learned controller are sparse. There is also a conceptual gap left unaddressed: treating the evolving training process as an RL MDP assumes sufficient stationarity for actor–critic updates, but the paper does not analyze or justify this non‑stationarity/instability. Overall the methods and derivations are reasonable, but empirical support and discussion of key assumptions are incomplete.
